    21→open Filter
    22→
    23→/-- Say a sequence is lacunary if there exists some $\lambda > 1$ such that
    24→$a_{n+1}/a_n > \lambda$ for all sufficiently large $n$. -/
    25→def IsLacunary (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop := ∃ c > (1 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ k in atTop, c * n k < n (k + 1)
    26→
    27→/-- Every lacunary sequence is eventually strictly increasing. -/
    28→lemma IsLacunary.eventually_lt {n : ℕ → ℕ} (hn : IsLacunary n) : ∀ᶠ k in atTop, n k < n (k + 1) := by
    29→  obtain ⟨c, hc, h⟩ := hn
    30→  obtain ⟨N, h⟩ := eventually_atTop.1 h
    31→  refine eventually_atTop.2 ⟨N, fun b hb ↦ ?_⟩
    32→  grw [hc] at h
    33→  simpa using h _ hb
    34→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 995
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/995](https://www.erdosproblems.com/995)
    23→
    24→[Er49d] Erdős, P. (1949) — constructed a lacunary sequence and $f \in L^2([0,1])$ such that, for
    25→every $\varepsilon > 0$, for almost all $\alpha$,
    26→$\limsup_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N(\log \log N)^{1/2 - \varepsilon}} \sum_{k \leq N}
    27→f(\{\alpha n_k\}) = \infty$.
    28→
    29→[Er64b] Erdős, P. (1964) — thought that his lower bound was closer to the truth.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open MeasureTheory Filter Finset Set
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos995
    35→
    36→/-- A sequence of positive integers is lacunary if it is strictly increasing
    37→and there exists $q > 1$ such that $n_{k+1} \geq q \cdot n_k$ for all $k$. -/
    38→def IsLacunary (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop :=
    39→  StrictMono n ∧ ∃ q : ℝ, q > 1 ∧ ∀ k : ℕ, (n k : ℝ) * q ≤ n (k + 1)
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 995 [Er64b]:
    43→
    44→Is it true that for every lacunary sequence $(n_k)$ of positive integers and every
    45→$f \in L^2([0,1])$, for almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$,
    46→$$\sum_{k < N} f(\{\alpha \cdot n_k\}) = o(N \cdot \sqrt{\log \log N})?$$
    47→
    48→Formulated as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for almost all $\alpha$, eventually (for large $N$),
    49→$\left|\sum_{k < N} f(\{\alpha \cdot n_k\})\right| \leq \varepsilon \cdot N \cdot
    50→\sqrt{\log \log N}$.
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 11 42]
    53→theorem erdos_995 : answer(sorry) ↔
    54→    ∀ (n : ℕ → ℕ), IsLacunary n →
    55→    ∀ (f : ℝ → ℝ), Measurable f →
    56→    Integrable (fun x => f x ^ 2) (volume.restrict (Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)) →
    57→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    58→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
    59→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ N : ℕ, N ≥ N₀ →
    60→        |∑ k ∈ Finset.range N, f (Int.fract (α * (n k : ℝ)))| ≤
    61→          ε * (N : ℝ) * Real.sqrt (Real.log (Real.log (N : ℝ))) := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→end Erdos995
    65→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 996
    21→
    22→*Reference:*
    23→ - [erdosproblems.com/996](https://www.erdosproblems.com/996)
    24→ - [Er49d] Erdös, P. "On the strong law of large numbers." Transactions of the American Mathematical
    25→    Society 67.1 (1949): 51-56.
    26→ - [Ma66] Matsuyama, Noboru. "On the strong law of large numbers." Tohoku Mathematical Journal,
    27→    Second Series 18.3 (1966): 259-269.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open MeasureTheory AddCircle Filter Topology Asymptotics Finset Real
    31→
    32→noncomputable def fourierPartial {T : ℝ} [hT : Fact (0 < T)] (f : Lp ℂ 2 (@haarAddCircle T hT))
    33→    (k : ℕ) : AddCircle T → ℂ :=
    34→  fun x => ∑ i ∈ Icc (-k : ℤ) k, fourierCoeff f k • fourier i x
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos996
    37→
    38→/-- Does there exists a positive constant `C` such that for all `f ∈ L²[0,1]` and all lacunary
    39→sequences `n`, if `‖f - fₖ‖₂ = O(1 / log log log k ^ C)`, then for almost every `x`,
    40→`lim ∑ k ∈ Finset.range N, f (n k • x)) / N = ∫ t, f t ∂t`? -/
    41→@[category research open, AMS 42]
    42→theorem erdos_996.log3 : answer(sorry) ↔
    43→    ∃ (C : ℝ), 0 < C ∧ ∀ (f : Lp ℂ 2 (haarAddCircle (T := 1))) (n : ℕ → ℕ),
    44→    IsLacunary n →
    45→    (fun k => (eLpNorm (fourierPartial f k) 2 (haarAddCircle (T := 1))).toReal) =O[atTop]
    46→    (fun k => 1 / (log (log (log k))) ^ C)
    47→    →
    48→    ∀ᵐ x, Tendsto (fun N => (∑ k ∈ .range N, f (n k • x)) / N) atTop
    49→    (𝓝 (∫ t, f t ∂haarAddCircle)) := by
    50→  sorry
    51→
    52→/-- The following theorem is proved in [Ma66]. -/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 42]
    54→theorem erdos_996.log2 : ∀ (C : ℝ), 0.5 < C →
    55→    ∀ (f : Lp ℂ 2 (haarAddCircle (T := 1))) (n : ℕ → ℕ),
    56→    IsLacunary n →
    57→    (fun k => (eLpNorm (fourierPartial f k) 2 (haarAddCircle (T := 1))).toReal) =O[atTop]
    58→    (fun k => 1 / (log (log k)) ^ C)
    59→    →
    60→    ∀ᵐ x, Tendsto (fun N => (∑ k ∈ .range N, f (n k • x)) / N) atTop
    61→    (𝓝 (∫ t, f t ∂haarAddCircle)) := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→end Erdos996
    65→

    24→- [CLLW24] J. Champagne, T. Le, Y.-R. Liu, and T. D. Wooley, Well-distribution modulo one and the

    26→- [Er64b] Erdős, P., Problems and results on diophantine approximations. Compositio Math. (1964),
    27→  52-65.
    28→- [Er85e] Erdős, P., Some problems and results in number theory. Number theory and combinatorics.
    29→  Japan 1984 (Tokyo, Okayama and Kyoto, 1984) (1985), 65-87.
    30→- [Hl55] Hlawka, Edmund, Zur formalen {T}heorie der {G}leichverteilung in kompakten {G}ruppen. Rend.
    31→  Circ. Mat. Palermo (2) (1955), 33--47.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Set
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos997
    37→
    38→/--
    39→Call $x_1,x_2,\ldots \in (0,1)$ well-distributed if, for every $\epsilon>0$, if $k$ is
    40→sufficiently large then, for all $n>0$ and intervals $I\subseteq [0,1]$,
    41→$\lvert \# \{ n < m\leq n+k : x_m\in I\} - \lvert I\rvert k\rvert < \epsilon k.$
    42→
    43→The notion of a well-distributed sequence was introduced by Hlawka and Petersen [Hl55].
    44→-/
    45→def IsWellDistributed (x : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    46→  ∀ ε > 0, ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, ∀ n : ℕ,
    47→  ∀ a b, 0 ≤ a → a ≤ b → b ≤ 1 →
    48→    letI I := Ico a b
    49→    let count := (Finset.Ioc n (n + k)).filter (fun m ↦ x m ∈ I)
    50→    abs ((count.card : ℝ) - (b - a) * k) < ε * k
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Is it true that, for every $\alpha$, the sequence $\{ \alpha p_n\}$ is not well-distributed,
    54→if $p_n$ is the sequence of primes?
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    57→theorem erdos_997 :
    58→    answer(sorry) ↔
    59→      ∀ α : ℝ, ¬ IsWellDistributed (fun n ↦ Int.fract (α * (n.nth Nat.Prime))) := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős proved that, if $n_k$ is a lacunary sequence, then the sequence $\{ \alpha n_k\}$ is not
    64→well-distributed for almost all $\alpha$.
    65→-/
    66→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    67→theorem erdos_997.variants.lacunary (n : ℕ → ℕ) (h : IsLacunary n) :
    68→    ∀ᵐ α, ¬ IsWellDistributed (fun k ↦ Int.fract (α * (n k : ℝ))) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→/--
    72→He also claimed in [Er64b] to have proved that there exists an irrational $\alpha$ for which
    73→$\{\alpha p_n\}$ is not well-distributed. He later retracted this claim in [Er85e], saying "The
    74→theorem is no doubt correct and perhaps will not be difficult to prove but I never was able to
    75→reconstruct my 'proof' which perhaps never existed."
    76→
    77→The existence of such an $\alpha$ was established by Champagne, Le, Liu, and Wooley [CLLW24].
    78→-/
    79→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    80→theorem erdos_997.variants.irrational :
    81→    ∃ α : ℝ, Irrational α ∧
    82→      ¬ IsWellDistributed (fun n ↦ Int.fract (α * (n.nth Nat.Prime))) := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Erdos997
    86→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 992
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/992](https://www.erdosproblems.com/992)
    23→
    24→Let $x_1 < x_2 < \cdots$ be an infinite sequence of integers. Is it true that, for
    25→almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$, the discrepancy
    26→$$D(N) = \max_{I \subseteq [0,1]} |\#\{n \le N : \{\alpha x_n\} \in I\} - |I| \cdot N|$$
    27→satisfies $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{o(1)}$? Or even $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log \log N)^{O(1)}$?
    28→
    29→Erdős and Koksma [ErKo49] and Cassels [Ca50] independently proved that, for any
    30→sequence $x_i$ and almost all $\alpha$, $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{5/2 + o(1)}$. Baker [Ba81]
    31→improved this to $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{3/2 + o(1)}$.
    32→
    33→This was disproved by Berkes and Philipp [BePh94], who constructed a sequence
    34→of integers $x_1 < x_2 < \cdots$ such that, for almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$,
    35→$$\limsup_{N \to \infty} D(N) / (N \log N)^{1/2} > 0.$$
    36→-/
    37→
    38→open Filter MeasureTheory Finset
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos992
    41→
    42→/-- The discrepancy of the sequence $\{\alpha \cdot x(n)\} \pmod{1}$ with respect to
    43→subintervals of $[0,1)$.
    44→$$D(N) = \sup_{0 \le a < b \le 1} |\#\{n < N : \{\alpha \cdot x(n)\} \in [a, b)\} - (b - a) \cdot N|$$ -/
    45→noncomputable def discrepancy (x : ℕ → ℤ) (α : ℝ) (N : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    46→  sSup {d : ℝ | ∃ a b : ℝ, 0 ≤ a ∧ a < b ∧ b ≤ 1 ∧
    47→    d = abs (((Finset.range N).filter (fun n =>
    48→      a ≤ Int.fract (α * (x n : ℝ)) ∧ Int.fract (α * (x n : ℝ)) < b)).card -
    49→      (b - a) * (N : ℝ))}
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 992 (disproved by Berkes and Philipp [BePh94]):
    53→
    54→Is it true that for every strictly increasing sequence of positive integers
    55→$x_1 < x_2 < \cdots$ and almost all $\alpha \in [0,1]$, the discrepancy satisfies
    56→$D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{o(1)}$?
    57→
    58→Here $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log N)^{o(1)}$ is formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$,
    59→there exists $C > 0$ such that $D(N) \le C \sqrt{N} (\log N)^\varepsilon$ for all
    60→sufficiently large $N$.
    61→-/
    62→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    63→theorem erdos_992 : answer(False) ↔
    64→    ∀ (x : ℕ → ℤ), StrictMono x → (∀ n, 0 < x n) →
    65→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
    66→      ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    67→        ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    68→          ∀ᶠ N in atTop,
    69→            discrepancy x α N ≤
    70→              C * Real.sqrt (N : ℝ) * (Real.log (N : ℝ)) ^ ε := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→/--
    74→Erdős Problem 992, stronger variant (also disproved by [BePh94]):
    75→
    76→Is it true that $D(N) \ll N^{1/2} (\log \log N)^{O(1)}$? Here this is formalized as:
    77→there exist $C > 0$ and $K > 0$ such that $D(N) \le C \sqrt{N} (\log \log N)^K$ for
    78→all sufficiently large $N$.
    79→-/
    80→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    81→theorem erdos_992.variants.stronger : answer(False) ↔
    82→    ∀ (x : ℕ → ℤ), StrictMono x → (∀ n, 0 < x n) →
    83→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
    84→      ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧ ∃ K : ℝ, K > 0 ∧
    85→        ∀ᶠ N in atTop,
    86→          discrepancy x α N ≤
    87→            C * Real.sqrt (N : ℝ) *
    88→              (Real.log (Real.log (N : ℝ))) ^ K := by
    89→  sorry
    90→
    91→/--
    92→Berkes and Philipp [BePh94] disproved Erdős Problem 992 by constructing a
    93→strictly increasing sequence of positive integers such that for almost all
    94→$\alpha \in [0,1]$,
    95→$$\limsup_{N \to \infty} D(N) / (N \log N)^{1/2} > 0.$$
    96→-/
    97→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    98→theorem erdos_992.variants.berkes_philipp :
    99→    ∃ (x : ℕ → ℤ), StrictMono x ∧ (∀ n, 0 < x n) ∧
   100→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Icc (0 : ℝ) 1)),
   101→      ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
   102→        ∃ᶠ N in atTop,
   103→          discrepancy x α N ≥
   104→            c * Real.sqrt ((N : ℝ) * Real.log (N : ℝ)) := by
   105→  sorry
   106→
   107→end Erdos992
   108→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 994
    21→
    22→Khintchine's conjecture on strong uniform distribution: is it true that for almost all real
    23→numbers α, the fractional parts {kα} are equidistributed with respect to every measurable subset
    24→of (0,1)? Disproved by Marstrand [Ma70].
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/994](https://www.erdosproblems.com/994)
    27→
    28→[Ma70] Marstrand, J. M., _On Khintchine's conjecture about strong uniform distribution_.
    29→Proc. London Math. Soc. (3) 21 (1970), 540-556.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open scoped MeasureTheory
    33→
    34→open Filter Finset Set MeasureTheory
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos994
    37→
    38→/-- The Cesàro frequency of visits of the fractional parts $\{k\alpha\}$ to a set $E$,
    39→for $k = 1, \ldots, n$. That is, $\frac{1}{n} \cdot \#\{1 \le k \le n : \{k\alpha\} \in E\}$. -/
    40→noncomputable def cesaroFrequency (α : ℝ) (E : Set ℝ) (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    41→  have : DecidablePred (· ∈ E) := Classical.decPred _
    42→  ((Finset.range n).filter (fun (k : ℕ) =>
    43→    Int.fract (((k : ℝ) + 1) * α) ∈ E)).card / (n : ℝ)
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Erdős Problem 994 (Khintchine's conjecture, disproved by Marstrand [Ma70]):
    47→
    48→Let $E \subseteq (0,1)$ be a measurable subset with Lebesgue measure $\lambda(E)$. Is it true
    49→that, for almost all $\alpha$,
    50→$$
    51→  \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{1 \le k \le n} \mathbf{1}_{\{\{k\alpha\} \in E\}} = \lambda(E)
    52→$$
    53→for all $E$?
    54→
    55→This is false, and was disproved by Marstrand [Ma70].
    56→-/
    57→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    58→theorem erdos_994 : answer(False) ↔
    59→    ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume : Measure ℝ),
    60→      ∀ (E : Set ℝ), MeasurableSet E → E ⊆ Ioo 0 1 →
    61→        Tendsto (cesaroFrequency α E) atTop
    62→          (nhds (volume E).toReal) := by
    63→  sorry
    64→
    65→end Erdos994
    66→

    19→/-!

    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→ - [erdosproblems.com/346](https://www.erdosproblems.com/346)
    24→ - [Gr64d] Graham, R. L., A property of Fibonacci numbers. Fibonacci Quart. (1964), 1-10.
    25→ - [ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    26→    theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).

    29→
    30→open Filter Topology Set
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos346
    33→
    34→/-- Is it true that for every lacunary, strongly complete sequence `A` that is not complete whenever
    35→infinitely many terms are removed from it, `lim A (n + 1) / A n = (1 + √5) / 2`? -/
    36→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    37→theorem erdos_346 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ {A : ℕ → ℕ}, IsLacunary A → IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A →
    38→    (∀ B : Set ℕ, B ⊆ range A → B.Infinite → ¬ IsAddComplete (range A \ B)) →
    39→    Tendsto (fun n => A (n + 1) / (A n : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 ((1 + √5) / 2)) := by
    40→  sorry
    41→
    42→/-- We define a sequence `f` by the formula `f n = n.fib - (- 1) ^ n`. -/
    43→def f (n : ℕ) : ℕ := if Even n then n.fib - 1 else n.fib + 1
    44→
    45→/-- The sequence `f` is lacunary. -/
    46→@[category test, AMS 11]
    47→theorem erdos_346.f_isLacunary : IsLacunary f := by sorry
    48→
    49→/-- The sequence `f` is strongly complete, and this is proved in [Gr64d]. -/
    50→@[category test, AMS 11]
    51→theorem erdos_346.f_isAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq f := by sorry
    52→
    53→/-- The sequence `f` is not complete whenever infinitely many terms are removed from it, and this
    54→is proved in [Gr64d]. -/
    55→@[category test, AMS 11]
    56→theorem erdos_346.f_not_isAddComplete {B : Set ℕ} (h : B ⊆ range f) (hB : B.Infinite) :
    57→    ¬ IsAddComplete (range f \ B) := by
    58→  sorry
    59→
    60→/-- Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] remark that it is easy to see that if `A (n + 1) / A n > (1 + √5) / 2`
    61→then the second property is automatically satisfied. -/
    62→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    63→theorem erdos_346.gt_goldenRatio_not_IsAddComplete {A : ℕ → ℕ}
    64→    (hA : ∀ n, (1 + √5) / 2 * A n < A (n + 1)) {B : Set ℕ} (h : B ⊆ range A) (hB : B.Infinite) :
    65→    ¬ IsAddComplete (range A \ B) := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→/-- Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] also say that it is not hard to construct very irregular sequences
    69→satisfying the aforementioned properties. -/
    70→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    71→theorem erdos_346.example : ∃ A : ℕ → ℕ, IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A ∧

    74→    limsup (fun n => A (n + 1) / (A n : ENNReal)) atTop = ⊤ := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→end Erdos346

    20→/-!

    22→
    23→*References:*

    31→/--
    32→Is there a lacunary sequence $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ (so that $A=\{a_1 < \cdots\}$ and
    33→there exists some $\lambda > 1$ such that $a_{n+1}/a_n\geq \lambda$ for all $n\geq 1$) such that
    34→\[\left\{ \sum_{a\in A'}\frac{1}{a} : A'\subseteq A\textrm{ finite}\right\}\]
    35→contain all rationals in some open interval?
    36→
    37→Bleicher and Erdős conjectured the answer is no.
    38→
    39→In fact the answer is yes, with any lacunarity constant $\lambda\in (1,2)$ (though not $\lambda=2$),
    40→as proved by van Doorn and Kova\v{c} [DoKo25].

    23→/-- Say a sequence is lacunary if there exists some $\lambda > 1$ such that
    24→$a_{n+1}/a_n > \lambda$ for all sufficiently large $n$. -/
    25→def IsLacunary (n : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop := ∃ c > (1 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ k in atTop, c * n k < n (k + 1)
    26→
    27→/-- Every lacunary sequence is eventually strictly increasing. -/
    28→lemma IsLacunary.eventually_lt {n : ℕ → ℕ} (hn : IsLacunary n) : ∀ᶠ k in atTop, n k < n (k + 1) := by

    30→  obtain ⟨N, h⟩ := eventually_atTop.1 h
    31→  refine eventually_atTop.2 ⟨N, fun b hb ↦ ?_⟩
    32→  grw [hc] at h
    33→  simpa using h _ hb

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 526
    21→
    22→Dvoretzky's covering problem: given a sequence of arc lengths $a_n \to 0$ with $\sum a_n = \infty$,
    23→arcs of these lengths placed independently and uniformly at random on the unit circle cover the
    24→entire circle with probability one if and only if $\sum \exp(a_1+\cdots+a_n)/n^2 = \infty$.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/526](https://www.erdosproblems.com/526)
    27→
    28→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. 6 (1961),
    29→p. 253.
    30→
    31→[Sh72] Shepp, L. A., _Covering the circle with random arcs_. Israel J. Math. 11 (1972), 328–345.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open MeasureTheory ProbabilityTheory Filter Finset BigOperators Set
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos526
    37→
    38→/-- Point $y$ is covered by an arc of length $\ell$ starting at $x$ on the unit circle
    39→$\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$. The clockwise arc-distance from $x$ to $y$ is the fractional
    40→part of $(y - x)$. -/
    41→noncomputable def CircArcCovers (x y ℓ : ℝ) : Prop :=
    42→  Int.fract (y - x) < ℓ
    43→
    44→/-- The full unit circle $[0,1)$ is covered by arcs at centers $\omega(n)$ of lengths $a(n)$. -/
    45→noncomputable def CircleFullyCovered (ω a : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    46→  ∀ y : ℝ, 0 ≤ y → y < 1 → ∃ n : ℕ, CircArcCovers (ω n) y (a n)
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Dvoretzky's covering problem [Er61, p. 253] (solved by Shepp [Sh72]).
    50→
    51→Let $a_n \geq 0$ with $a_n \to 0$ and $\sum a_n = \infty$. If we independently and uniformly
    52→place random arcs of length $a_n$ on the unit circle, Shepp showed the circle is covered with
    53→probability $1$ if and only if
    54→$$
    55→  \sum_n \frac{\exp(a_1 + \cdots + a_n)}{n^2} = \infty.
    56→$$
    57→
    58→The probability space $(\Omega, \mu)$ carries independent $\mathrm{Uniform}[0,1)$ random
    59→variables $X(n)$ giving the starting point of the $n$-th arc.
    60→-/
    61→@[category research solved, AMS 60]
    62→theorem erdos_526
    63→    {Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω] {μ : Measure Ω} [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    64→    (a : ℕ → ℝ)
    65→    (ha_nonneg : ∀ n, 0 ≤ a n)
    66→    (ha_tendsto : Tendsto a atTop (nhds 0))
    67→    (ha_diverges : ¬Summable a)
    68→    {X : ℕ → Ω → ℝ}
    69→    (hX_meas : ∀ n, Measurable (X n))
    70→    (hX_unif : ∀ n, Measure.map (X n) μ = volume.restrict (Ico (0 : ℝ) 1))
    71→    (hX_indep : iIndepFun X μ) :
    72→    (∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, CircleFullyCovered (fun n => X n ω) a) ↔
    73→      ¬Summable (fun n => Real.exp (∑ i ∈ range (n + 1), a i) / ((n : ℝ) + 1) ^ 2) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→end Erdos526
    77→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1132
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1132](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1132)
    23→
    24→For $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in [-1,1]$, define the Lagrange basis polynomials
    25→$$
    26→  \ell_k(x) = \prod_{i \neq k} \frac{x - x_i}{x_k - x_i},
    27→$$
    28→so that $\ell_k(x_k) = 1$ and $\ell_k(x_i) = 0$ for $i \neq k$.
    29→
    30→Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots \in [-1,1]$ be an infinite sequence, and let
    31→$$
    32→  L_n(x) = \sum_{1 \leq k \leq n} |\ell_k(x)|,
    33→$$
    34→where each $\ell_k(x)$ is defined with respect to $x_1, \ldots, x_n$.
    35→
    36→[Be31] Bernstein proved that the set of $x \in (-1,1)$ for which the limsup
    37→condition (Part 2) holds is everywhere dense.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open Finset BigOperators Filter MeasureTheory
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos1132
    43→
    44→/-- The Lagrange basis polynomial $\ell_k(x)$ for nodes indexed by $\operatorname{Fin} n$.
    45→$\ell_k(x) = \prod_{i \neq k} \frac{x - x_i}{x_k - x_i}$ -/
    46→noncomputable def lagrangeBasis {n : ℕ} (nodes : Fin n → ℝ) (k : Fin n) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    47→  ∏ i ∈ univ.filter (· ≠ k), (x - nodes i) / (nodes k - nodes i)
    48→
    49→/-- The Lebesgue function: $L_n(x) = \sum_k |\ell_k(x)|$ -/
    50→noncomputable def lebesgueFunction {n : ℕ} (nodes : Fin n → ℝ) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    51→  ∑ k, |lagrangeBasis nodes k x|
    52→
    53→/-- The first $n$ elements of an infinite sequence, viewed as $\operatorname{Fin} n \to \mathbb{R}$. -/
    54→noncomputable def firstN (seq : ℕ → ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Fin n → ℝ := fun i => seq i.val
    55→
    56→/-- $L_n(x)$: the Lebesgue function using the first $n$ points of the sequence. -/
    57→noncomputable def L (seq : ℕ → ℝ) (n : ℕ) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    58→  lebesgueFunction (firstN seq n) x
    59→
    60→/-- A sequence is valid for interpolation: values in $[-1,1]$ and pairwise distinct. -/
    61→def ValidSeq (seq : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    62→  Function.Injective seq ∧ ∀ i, seq i ∈ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Erdős Problem 1132 (Part 1):
    66→
    67→For any infinite sequence $x_1, x_2, \ldots \in [-1,1]$ of distinct points, must there
    68→exist $x \in (-1,1)$ and a constant $C$ such that $L_n(x) > \frac{2}{\pi} \log n - C$ for
    69→infinitely many $n$?
    70→-/
    71→@[category research open, AMS 26 41]
    72→theorem erdos_1132 : answer(sorry) ↔
    73→    ∀ (seq : ℕ → ℝ), ValidSeq seq →
    74→      ∃ x ∈ Set.Ioo (-1 : ℝ) 1, ∃ C : ℝ,
    75→        ∃ᶠ n in atTop,
    76→          L seq n x > (2 / Real.pi) * Real.log (n : ℝ) - C := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→/--
    80→Erdős Problem 1132 (Part 2):
    81→
    82→For any infinite sequence $x_1, x_2, \ldots \in [-1,1]$ of distinct points, is it true that
    83→$\limsup_{n \to \infty} L_n(x) / \log n \geq \frac{2}{\pi}$
    84→for almost all $x \in (-1,1)$?
    85→
    86→Bernstein [Be31] proved that the set of $x \in (-1,1)$ for which the limsup
    87→condition holds is everywhere dense.
    88→-/
    89→@[category research open, AMS 26 41]
    90→theorem erdos_1132.variants.part2 : answer(sorry) ↔
    91→    ∀ (seq : ℕ → ℝ), ValidSeq seq →
    92→      ∀ᵐ x ∂(volume.restrict (Set.Ioo (-1 : ℝ) 1)),
    93→        Filter.limsup (fun n => L seq n x / Real.log (n : ℝ)) atTop ≥ 2 / Real.pi := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→end Erdos1132
    97→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1152
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1152](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1152)
    23→
    24→For $n \geq 1$ fix some sequence of $n$ distinct numbers
    25→$x_{1,n}, \ldots, x_{n,n} \in [-1,1]$. Let $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(n) \to 0$.
    26→
    27→Does there always exist a continuous function $f : [-1,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ such that if
    28→$p_n$ is a sequence of polynomials, with degrees $\deg p_n < (1 + \varepsilon(n))n$, such
    29→that $p_n(x_{k,n}) = f(x_{k,n})$ for all $1 \leq k \leq n$, then $p_n(x) \not\to f(x)$
    30→for almost all $x \in [-1,1]$?
    31→
    32→Erdős, Kroó, and Szabados [EKS89] proved that if $\varepsilon > 0$ is fixed (does not
    33→$\to 0$), then there exist sequences $x_{i,j}$ such that for any continuous function $f$,
    34→there exists a sequence of polynomials $p_n$ with $\deg p_n < (1+\varepsilon)n$ interpolating
    35→$f$ at $x_{k,n}$, and $p_n(x) \to f(x)$ uniformly for all $x \in [-1,1]$.
    36→
    37→[EKS89] Erdős, P., Kroó, A., and Szabados, J., _On convergent interpolatory polynomials_
    38→(1989).
    39→
    40→[Va99] Vértesi, P., _Classical (unweighted) and weighted interpolation_ (1999).
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open Filter Polynomial MeasureTheory Set
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos1152
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Erdős Problem 1152 [Va99, 2.42]:
    49→
    50→For any triangular array of distinct interpolation nodes in $[-1,1]$ and any function
    51→$\varepsilon(n) \to 0$, there exists a continuous function $f : [-1,1] \to \mathbb{R}$
    52→such that every sequence of polynomials $p_n$ with $\deg p_n < (1 + \varepsilon(n))n$
    53→interpolating $f$ at the nodes fails to converge to $f$ for almost every $x \in [-1,1]$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research open, AMS 41]
    56→theorem erdos_1152 : answer(sorry) ↔
    57→    ∀ (x : (n : ℕ) → Fin n → ℝ),
    58→      (∀ n, ∀ k : Fin n, x n k ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) →
    59→      (∀ n, Function.Injective (x n)) →
    60→      ∀ (ε : ℕ → ℝ),
    61→        (∀ n, 0 < ε n) →
    62→        Tendsto ε atTop (nhds 0) →
    63→        ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, ContinuousOn f (Icc (-1) 1) ∧
    64→          ∀ p : ℕ → Polynomial ℝ,
    65→            (∀ n, n ≥ 1 → ((p n).natDegree : ℝ) < (1 + ε n) * n) →
    66→            (∀ n, ∀ k : Fin n, (p n).eval (x n k) = f (x n k)) →
    67→            ∀ᵐ t ∂(volume.restrict (Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1)),
    68→              ¬Tendsto (fun n => (p n).eval t) atTop (nhds (f t)) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos1152
    72→

    20→
    21→variable {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M]
    22→
    23→open scoped List
    24→
    25→/-- The set of subset sums of a set `A ⊆ M`. -/
    26→def subsetSums (A : Set M) : Set M :=
    27→  {n | ∃ B : Finset M, ↑B ⊆ A ∧ n = ∑ i ∈ B, i}
    28→
    29→/-- If `A ⊆ B`, then `subsetSums A ⊆ subsetSums B`. -/
    30→@[gcongr]
    31→theorem subsetSums_mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) : subsetSums A ⊆ subsetSums B :=
    32→  fun _ ⟨C, hC⟩ => ⟨C, hC.1.trans h, hC.2⟩
    33→
    34→/-- The set of subset sums of a sequence `ℕ → M`, where repetition is allowed. -/
    35→def subseqSums' (A : ℕ → M) : Set M :=
    36→  {n | ∃ B : Finset ℕ, n = ∑ i ∈ B, A i}
    37→
    38→variable [Preorder M]
    39→
    40→/-- A set `A ⊆ M` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a subset sum of `A`. -/
    41→def IsAddComplete (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    42→  ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, k ∈ subsetSums A
    43→
    44→/-- If `A ⊆ B` and `A` is complete, then `B` is also complete. -/
    45→@[gcongr]
    46→theorem IsAddComplete.mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) (ha : IsAddComplete A) : IsAddComplete B := by
    47→  filter_upwards [ha] with x hx
    48→  exact (subsetSums_mono h) hx
    49→
    50→/-- A set `A ⊆ M` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a subset sum of `A`. -/
    51→def IsAddStronglyComplete (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    52→  ∀ ⦃B : Set M⦄, B.Finite → IsAddComplete (A \ B)
    53→
    54→/-- A strongly complete set is complete. -/
    55→theorem IsAddStronglyComplete.isAddComplete {A : Set M} (hA : IsAddStronglyComplete A) :
    56→    IsAddComplete A := by simpa using hA Set.finite_empty
    57→
    58→/-- If `A ⊆ B` and `A` is strongly complete, then `B` is also strongly complete. -/
    59→theorem IsAddStronglyComplete.mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) (ha : IsAddStronglyComplete A) :
    60→    IsAddStronglyComplete B := fun C hC => (ha hC).mono (by grind)
    61→
    62→/-- A sequence `A` is strongly complete if `fun m => A (n + m)` is still complete for all `n`. -/
    63→def IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq (A : ℕ → M) : Prop :=
    64→  ∀ n, IsAddComplete (Set.range (fun m => A (n + m)))
    65→
    66→/-- A strongly complete sequence is complete. -/
    67→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.isAddComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    68→    (hA : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A) :
    69→    IsAddComplete (Set.range A) := by simpa using hA 0
    70→
    71→open Classical in
    72→/-- If the range of a sequence `A` is strongly complete, then `A` is strongly complete. -/
    73→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.of_isAddStronglyComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    74→    (h : IsAddStronglyComplete (.range A)) : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A :=
    75→  fun n => (h (Finset.finite_toSet _)).mono (A := .range A \ ((Finset.range n).image A))
    76→    (fun _ ⟨⟨y, hy⟩, q⟩ => ⟨y - n, by grind⟩)
    77→
    78→/-- If `A` is strongly complete and the preimage of each element is finite, then the range of `A`
    79→is strongly complete. -/
    80→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.isAddStronglyComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    81→    (h : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A) (hA : ∀ m, (A ⁻¹' {m}).Finite) :
    82→    IsAddStronglyComplete (.range A) := by
    83→  refine fun B hB => ?_
    84→  obtain ⟨n, hn⟩ := Finset.exists_nat_subset_range (hB.preimage' (fun b _ => hA b)).toFinset
    85→  rw [Set.Finite.toFinset_subset, Finset.coe_range] at hn
    86→  refine (h (n + 1)).mono ?_
    87→  refine fun x ⟨y, hy⟩ => ⟨⟨n + 1 + y, hy⟩, fun hx => ?_⟩
    88→  have : n + 1 + y ∈ Set.Iio n := by grind
    89→  grind
    90→
    91→/-- A sequence `A` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a sum of
    92→(not necessarily distinct) terms of `A`. -/
    93→def IsAddCompleteNatSeq' (A : ℕ → M) : Prop :=
    94→  ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, k ∈ subseqSums' A

    27→namespace Set
    28→
    29→/--
    30→Given a set `S` and an element `b` in an order `β`, where all intervals bounded above are finite,
    31→we define the partial density of `S` (relative to a set `A`) to be the proportion of elements in
    32→`{x ∈ A | x < b}` that lie in `S ∩ A`.
    33→
    34→This definition was inspired from https://github.com/b-mehta/unit-fractions
    35→-/
    36→@[inline]
    37→noncomputable abbrev partialDensity {β : Type*} [Preorder β] [LocallyFiniteOrderBot β]
    38→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) (b : β) : ℝ :=
    39→  (Set.interIio (S ∩ A) b).ncard / (Set.interIio A b).ncard
    40→
    41→theorem partialDensity_le_one {β : Type*} [Preorder β] [LocallyFiniteOrderBot β]
    42→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) (b : β) : S.partialDensity A b ≤ 1 := by
    43→  apply div_le_one_of_le₀ _ (Nat.cast_nonneg _)
    44→  exact mod_cast Set.ncard_le_ncard <| Set.inter_subset_inter_left _ inter_subset_right
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Given a set `S` in an order `β`, where all intervals bounded above are finite, we define the upper
    48→density of `S` (relative to a set `A`) to be the limsup of the partial densities of `S`
    49→(relative to `A`) for `b → ∞`.
    50→-/
    51→noncomputable def upperDensity {β : Type*} [Preorder β] [LocallyFiniteOrderBot β]
    52→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) : ℝ :=
    53→  atTop.limsup fun (b : β) ↦ S.partialDensity A b

    57→density of `S` (relative to a set `A`) to be the liminf of the partial densities of `S`
    58→(relative to `A`) for `b → ∞`.

    61→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) : ℝ :=

    64→theorem lowerDensity_le_one {β : Type*} [Preorder β] [LocallyFiniteOrderBot β]
    65→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) : S.lowerDensity A ≤ 1 := by
    66→  by_cases h : atTop (α := β) = ⊥
    67→  · simp [h, Set.lowerDensity, Filter.liminf_eq]
    68→  · have : (atTop (α := β)).NeBot := ⟨h⟩
    69→    apply Real.sSup_le (fun x hx ↦ ?_) one_pos.le
    70→    simpa using hx.mono fun y hy ↦ hy.trans (Set.partialDensity_le_one _ _ y)

    74→  rw [Set.lowerDensity, Filter.liminf_eq]

    80→density `α : ℝ` (relative to a set `A`) if the proportion of `x ∈ S` such that `x < n`
    81→in `A` tends to `α` as `n → ∞`.

    84→(i.e., relative to all of `ℕ`).

   185→section LogarithmicDensity
   186→
   187→open Finset Real Classical
   188→
   189→/--
   190→A set `A` of natural numbers has logarithmic density `d` if the sequence
   191→$(1 / \log n) \cdot \sum_{k \in A, k \le n} (1/k)$ converges to `d`.
   192→Logarithmic density is a weaker notion than natural density: if a set has natural density `d`,
   193→then it also has logarithmic density `d` (see `Set.HasDensity.hasLogDensity`), but the converse
   194→is false.
   195→-/
   196→def Set.HasLogDensity (A : Set ℕ) (d : ℝ) : Prop :=
   197→  Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (∑ k ≤ n with k ∈ A, (k : ℝ)⁻¹ / .log n : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 d)

   200→proof_wanted Set.HasDensity.hasLogDensity {A : Set ℕ} {d : ℝ} (h : A.HasDensity d) : A.HasLogDensity d

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 991
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/991](https://www.erdosproblems.com/991)
    23→
    24→Suppose $A = \{w_1, \ldots, w_n\} \subset S^2$ maximises $\prod_{i<j} |w_i - w_j|$ over all
    25→possible sets of size $n$ (such sets are called Fekete points).
    26→
    27→Is it true that
    28→$$
    29→  \max_C \left| |A \cap C| - \alpha_C \cdot n \right| = o(n),
    30→$$
    31→where the maximum is taken over all spherical caps $C$ and $\alpha_C$ is the area
    32→of $C$ (normalised so that the entire sphere has area $1$)?
    33→
    34→This was proved. Brauchart [Br08] showed $O(n^{3/4})$ and Marzo–Mas [MaMa21]
    35→improved this to $O(n^{2/3})$.
    36→
    37→[Br08] Brauchart, J. S., *Optimal discrete Riesz energy and discrepancy*,
    38→Uniform Distribution Theory 3 (2008), no. 1, 47–78.
    39→
    40→[MaMa21] Marzo, J. and Mas, A., *Discrepancy of minimal Riesz energy points*,
    41→J. Approx. Theory 264 (2021), 105538.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open Classical Finset BigOperators
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos991
    47→
    48→/-- The product of pairwise distances of a finite set of points. -/
    49→noncomputable def pairwiseDistProd (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))) : ℝ :=
    50→  ∏ p ∈ P.offDiag, ‖p.1 - p.2‖
    51→
    52→/-- A finite set of points on $S^2$ maximises the pairwise distance product
    53→among all sets of the same cardinality on $S^2$. -/
    54→def IsMaxPairwiseDist (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))) : Prop :=
    55→  (∀ p ∈ P, ‖p‖ = 1) ∧
    56→  ∀ (Q : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))),
    57→    (∀ q ∈ Q, ‖q‖ = 1) → Q.card = P.card →
    58→    pairwiseDistProd Q ≤ pairwiseDistProd P
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 991 [Er64b]:
    62→
    63→If $A = \{w_1, \ldots, w_n\} \subset S^2$ are Fekete points (maximising the product of
    64→pairwise distances), then the spherical cap discrepancy of $A$ is $o(n)$:
    65→for every $\varepsilon > 0$, if $n$ is large enough and $P$ is any set of $n$ Fekete points,
    66→then for every spherical cap $C(v,t) = \{x \in S^2 : \langle x,v \rangle \geq t\}$,
    67→$$
    68→  \left| |P \cap C(v,t)| - \frac{1-t}{2} \cdot n \right| \leq \varepsilon \cdot n.
    69→$$
    70→
    71→Here $\frac{1-t}{2}$ is the normalised surface area of the cap $C(v,t)$ on $S^2$.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research solved, AMS 31 52]
    74→theorem erdos_991 :
    75→    answer(True) ↔
    76→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    77→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))),
    78→      IsMaxPairwiseDist P →
    79→      P.card ≥ N₀ →
    80→      ∀ (v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)) (t : ℝ),
    81→        ‖v‖ = 1 → -1 ≤ t → t ≤ 1 →
    82→        |((P.filter (fun x => @inner ℝ _ _ x v ≥ t)).card : ℝ) -
    83→          (1 - t) / 2 * (P.card : ℝ)| ≤ ε * (P.card : ℝ) := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→end Erdos991

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 998
    21→
    22→Is it true that if, for an irrational $\alpha$, the number of $m \leq n$ with
    23→$\{\alpha m\} \in [u,v)$ is $n(v-u) + O(1)$, then $u$ and $v$ must be fractional
    24→parts of integer multiples of $\alpha$? Proved by Kesten.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/998](https://www.erdosproblems.com/998)
    27→
    28→[Ke66] Kesten, H., _On a conjecture of Erdős and Szüsz related to uniform distribution mod 1_.
    29→Acta Arithmetica (1966), 193-212.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Classical Finset
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos998
    35→
    36→/--
    37→The number of integers $m$ with $1 \leq m \leq n$ such that the fractional part of $\alpha \cdot m$
    38→lies in $[u, v)$.
    39→-/
    40→noncomputable def countFracInInterval (α : ℝ) (u v : ℝ) (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    41→  ((Finset.Icc 1 n).filter (fun m : ℕ =>
    42→    u ≤ Int.fract (α * ↑m) ∧ Int.fract (α * ↑m) < v)).card
    43→
    44→/--
    45→Let $\alpha$ be an irrational number. Is it true that if, for all large $n$,
    46→$$\#\{1 \leq m \leq n : \{\alpha m\} \in [u,v)\} = n(v-u) + O(1)$$
    47→then $u = \{\alpha k\}$ and $v = \{\alpha \ell\}$ for some integers $k$ and $\ell$?
    48→
    49→Here $\{x\}$ denotes the fractional part of $x$. This is a problem of Erdős and
    50→Szüsz. The converse was proved by Hecke and Ostrowski. The conjecture itself
    51→was proved by Kesten [Ke66].
    52→-/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    54→theorem erdos_998 : answer(True) ↔
    55→    ∀ (α : ℝ), Irrational α →
    56→    ∀ (u v : ℝ), 0 ≤ u → u < v → v ≤ 1 →
    57→    (∃ C : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, 0 < n →
    58→      |(↑(countFracInInterval α u v n) : ℝ) - ↑n * (v - u)| ≤ C) →
    59→    (∃ k : ℤ, u = Int.fract (α * ↑k)) ∧ (∃ ℓ : ℤ, v = Int.fract (α * ↑ℓ)) := by
    60→  sorry

    62→end Erdos998
